content:
4 min read Just now -- Most React developers learn useEffect incorrectly. They are told: • componentDidMount → useEffect with [] • componentDidUpdate → useEffect with dependencies • componentWillUnmount → cleanup function While technically true, this mental model is responsible for thousands of bugs in modern React applications. The problem is simple: useEffect is not a lifecycle hook. It’s a synchronization hook. And once you understand that distinction, everything about useEffect starts making sense. Press enter or click to view image in full size The Biggest Misconception About useEffect When developers think about lifecycle methods, they ask: “When should this code run?” React asks a different question: “What external system should this component stay synchronized with?” That difference changes everything. Consider this example: useEffect(() => { document.title = title; }, [title]);Most developers think: Run this effect whenever title changes. React thinks: Keep the browser tab title synchronized with React state. That’s the real purpose of useEffect. Not lifecycle management. Synchronization. Press enter or click to view image in full size What Exactly Is an Effect? An effect is any operation that reaches outside React’s rendering system. Examples: ✓ API requests ✓ Browser APIs ✓ WebSocket connections ✓ Analytics tracking ✓ Local Storage ✓ Timers ✓ DOM subscriptions Examples that are NOT effects: const fullName = `${firstName} ${lastName}`;const filteredUsers = users.filter(user => user.active);const total = items.reduce(sumReducer, 0);These are computations. Not side effects. Yet many codebases incorrectly place them inside useEffect. The Hidden Cost of Misusing useEffect I frequently review codebases where developers write: const [fullName, setFullName] = useState("");useEffect(() => { setFullName( `${firstName} ${lastName}` ); }, [firstName, lastName]);This introduces: • Additional render cycles • More state management • More complexity • More bugs Instead: const fullName = `${firstName} ${lastName}`;No effect. No state. No problem. A useful rule: If you can calculate it during render, you probably don’t need useEffect. Understanding React’s Render Cycle To master useEffect, you need to understand one thing: React rendering must remain pure. When React renders: function UserProfile() { return
Hello
; }React expects: • No network requests • No timers • No subscriptions • No mutations Rendering should be predictable. Effects exist because some operations cannot happen during render. React first renders. Then React commits changes to the DOM. Only after that does React execute effects. This is why useEffect runs after rendering. Not before. The Dependency Array Is Not an Optimization Tool Many developers treat dependencies like performance tuning. Example: useEffect(() => { fetchUser(userId); }, []);Looks harmless. But it’s wrong. The effect uses userId. The dependency array says it doesn’t. You’ve created a synchronization bug. React now has stale information. The dependency array isn’t telling React: When should this run? It’s telling React: What data does this effect depend on? That’s a completely different conversation. Why Infinite Loops Happen One of the most common questions: Why is my useEffect running forever? Example: useEffect(() => { setCount(count + 1); }, [count]);Let’s follow the chain: • Effect runs • State changes • Component re-renders • Dependency changes • Effect runs again React is doing exactly what you asked. The bug isn’t React. The bug is the synchronization logic. Before writing an effect, ask: What external system am I synchronizing with? If the answer is: Another piece of React state You’re probably doing something wrong. Data Fetching: The Production Reality Most tutorials teach: useEffect(() => { fetch("/api/users") .then(res => res.json()) .then(setUsers); }, []);This works. Until production. Real applications face: • Slow networks • Component unmounts • Race conditions • Request cancellation • Duplicate requests A safer approach: useEffect(() => { const controller = new AbortController(); fetch("/api/users", { signal: controller.signal, }); return () => controller.abort(); }, []);Professional React development isn’t about making things work. It’s about making them resilient. Cleanup Is Not Optional Every subscription creates responsibility. Every listener creates responsibility. Every timer creates responsibility. Consider: useEffect(() => { const interval = setInterval(loadData, 5000); return () => clearInterval(interval); }, []);Without cleanup: • Memory leaks occur • Background work continues • Performance degrades A good engineer starts effects. A great engineer cleans them up. Senior-Level useEffect Rule Before writing an effect, ask three questions. Question 1 Can this be calculated during render? If yes: Don’t use useEffect. Question 2 Can this be moved into an event handler? If yes: Don’t use useEffect. Question 3 Am I synchronizing with something outside React? If no: Don’t use useEffect. The Future of React One interesting trend in modern React development: The best developers are writing fewer effects. Not more. With: • Server Components • React Query • RTK Query • Suspense • Framework-level data fetching Many traditional useEffect use cases are disappearing. The future isn’t mastering thousands of effects. It’s understanding when an effect should not exist at all. Final Thoughts The biggest breakthrough in my React journey came when I stopped asking: When should this effect run? And started asking: What am I synchronizing with? That single shift changed how I write React applications. Cleaner code. Fewer bugs. Better performance. More predictable behavior. Because useEffect was never designed to be a lifecycle replacement. It was designed to keep React connected to the outside world. And that’s a very different responsibility. The best useEffect is often the one you never had to write.
